chunk_summary: Guidelines for preventing and treating vitamin D deficiency and rickets in children emphasize supplementation, sunlight exposure, and dietary adequacy, with risk-based adjustments for high-risk groups.
learning_objectives: Understand the recommended vitamin D supplementation guidelines for children across different age groups.
Identify risk factors for vitamin D deficiency and rickets in children, including dietary and environmental factors.
Learn appropriate diagnostic thresholds for vitamin D deficiency and toxicity.
Recognize treatment regimens for vitamin D deficiency, including dosage and duration.
key_facts: Routine vitamin D supplementation is recommended at 400 IU/day for infants.
Older children and adolescents should meet vitamin D needs through sunlight and diet, with supplementation reserved for high-risk groups.
Deficiency is defined as serum 25(OH)D <12 ng/mL, insufficiency as 12–20 ng/mL, and sufficiency as >20 ng/mL.
Toxicity risk arises when serum 25(OH)D exceeds 100 ng/mL, often with hypercalcemia or hypercalciuria.
Treatment regimens include 2000 IU/day for infants <1 year and 3000 IU/day for older children for 12 weeks, alongside calcium supplementation.
reference_quote: Prevention: routine vitamin D supplementation is recommended in infancy at about 400 IU/day. For older children and adolescents, routine supplementation is not universally recommended; meeting requirements through sensible sunlight exposure and diet is preferred.
document_key_topics: Vitamin D supplementation guidelines for children
Diagnosis and treatment of vitamin D deficiency
